title: What a Skill is
subtitle: A folder of instructions that Claude loads on its own — when it's needed

theory.text: A familiar problem: you keep explaining the same thing

theory.md:
Picture this: every Monday you ask Claude to put together a report for your manager. And every time you explain it all over again: "this structure, business tone, numbers in a table, three takeaways at the end." A month later you've got a half-page prompt sitting in your notes that you copy and paste. It works, but it's clunky: the prompt is easy to forget, lose, or paste into the wrong chat.

This is exactly the problem **skills** (Agent Skills) solve. A skill is your instruction, packaged so that Claude finds and applies it on its own, with no copy-pasting. Describe once how to do a task correctly — and from then on you just say "make the report," and Claude already knows all your requirements.

theory.text: A skill is a folder with a SKILL.md file

theory.md:
Technically, a skill is very simple. It's a **folder** containing a `SKILL.md` file — an ordinary text file with instructions. It has two parts: a short "header" (the skill's name and a description of when to apply it) and the instructions themselves — what to do, in what order, what to avoid. Alongside `SKILL.md` you can put extra materials: document templates, examples, scripts, reference sheets.

No programming required: if you can write a note for a colleague, you can write a skill. We'll dig into the file's anatomy in detail in lesson three, but for now remember the main thing: a skill is knowledge shaped as a folder of instructions.

theory.title: What a skill looks like from the inside
theory.code:
weekly-report/
├── SKILL.md          ← main file: description + instructions
├── template.md       ← report template (optional)
└── examples/         ← examples of finished reports (optional)
    └── good-report.md

theory.text: The magic: Claude loads the skill itself

theory.md:
The key difference between a skill and a saved prompt is **automation**. You don't need to remember the skill and paste it into a chat. Claude sees the list of your skills — more precisely, their names and short descriptions — and when your request matches one skill's description, it opens the skill itself and follows the instructions.

This works like the shelf of reference books a good specialist keeps: they don't reread every book before each task — they glance at the spines and pull out the one they need. A skill's "spine" is its description. That's why the description is everything: it's what tells Claude when the skill is needed. There's a whole lesson devoted to this — lesson four.

An important bonus of this design: skills barely "eat up" space in the conversation. As long as a skill isn't needed, Claude keeps only its short description in mind, and reads the full instructions only when it takes on a matching task.

rows: A one-off message in a chat
A one-time or rare task: "translate," "explain," "come up with"
A space with shared context: instructions + files for one topic
Long work on one area: a specific product, course, client
A folder of instructions on "how to do a task," loaded automatically
A recurring type of task across any chats: reports, emails, formatting slides

theory.md:
The difference is simpler than it seems. A **prompt** answers "what to do right now." A **project** answers "what we're working on": it ties context to a topic, and the project's instructions only apply inside it. A **skill** answers "how to do this type of task correctly": it isn't tied to a topic and fires in any chat where a matching task comes up.

They combine beautifully. For example, you have a project "Launching an online store" with files about the products — and a skill "our email house style." You write in the project "draft an email to the supplier" — Claude takes context from the project and formats the email using the skill.

Another advantage of skills is portability. The same skill works everywhere Claude lives: in a chat on claude.ai, in Claude Code in the terminal, in apps built on the API. And you can share a skill: send a colleague a folder — and now they have the same "superpower." That's much harder to pull off with a prompt in your notes, and project instructions never leave the project's boundaries at all.

theory.bad: Pasting into the chat every single time: "You write posts for our bakery. Warm tone, no corporate speak, up to 400 characters, always one bread emoji, a question to readers at the end, hashtags #bakery #freshbread…" — and doing that before every post, in every new chat.
theory.good: Creating a bakery-posts skill once with those same rules inside SKILL.md. From then on, in any chat it's enough to write: "Make a post about the new croissants" — Claude applies all the skill's rules on its own.
theory.explanation: The content of the instructions is the same — the difference is in delivery. In the first case the rules live in your clipboard and are easily lost; in the second, in a skill that Claude finds automatically by its description. The more often a task recurs, the more a skill saves you.

theory.md: The Claude Help Center (the "Using skills" article) defines skills as folders of instructions, scripts, and resources that Claude loads when they're relevant to the current task. A key detail from the documentation: Claude uses skills **automatically** — it decides which skill to apply based on its description. Skills work in claude.ai, Claude Code, and via the API.

A simple "do I need a skill" test: caught yourself explaining the same thing to Claude for the third time? That's it. Anything you repeat is a skill candidate: report format, email style, table-formatting rules, a text-review checklist.

tasks.question: What is a skill from a technical point of view?
options: A separate neural network trained for a specific task
A folder with a SKILL.md file — instructions that Claude loads on its own
A paid plugin from an app store
A special command you have to enter at the start of every chat
tasks.explanation: A skill is just a folder containing a SKILL.md file with instructions (plus, optionally, templates and examples). No neural-network training and no commands: Claude finds the skill by its description on its own, when the task matches.
tasks.hint: Recall the picture of the weekly-report folder structure from the theory.

tasks.question: How is a skill fundamentally different from a saved prompt that you paste in manually?
options: A skill runs faster because it's written in a programming language
A skill can contain only code, while a prompt can contain only text
Claude loads the skill on its own, guided by its description — there's nothing to paste
tasks.explanation: The main difference is automation. A saved prompt has to be remembered, found, and pasted. Claude finds a skill on its own: it sees the names and descriptions of your skills and opens the matching one when a request lines up with the description.
tasks.hint: Recall the shelf-of-reference-books metaphor: the specialist pulls out the right book by glancing at the spines.

tasks.instruction: A colleague asks: "Prompt, project, skill — what's the difference?" Assemble an explanation from three blocks in a logical order: from one-off to permanent.
blocks: A prompt is a one-off message: "what to do right now." Good for one-time tasks.
A project is a space with context around one topic: "what we're working on." Its instructions apply inside it.
A skill is an instruction for "how to do this type of task": it loads on its own in any chat where a matching task comes up.
distractors: A skill is a paid subscription that speeds up Claude's responses.
A project and a skill can't be used together — you'll have to pick one.
tasks.explanation: The order follows the ladder from the lesson: prompt (one-off) → project (topic) → skill (task type, works everywhere). The trap blocks contain factual errors: a skill is not a subscription, and projects and skills combine beautifully — context from the project, rules from the skill.

tasks.question: Which of these situations are good candidates for creating a skill? (select all that apply)
options: Every Friday you ask Claude to gather the week's results in the same format
You need to translate one paragraph from English just this once
You constantly ask it to format sales proposals in your brand style
You want to find out the capital of Australia just this once
tasks.explanation: A skill pays off on recurring tasks with stable rules: weekly summaries and branded proposals are perfect candidates. A one-off translation and a question about a capital are ordinary prompts: there are no rules there worth saving.

tasks.instruction: A mini-practice. Imagine you already have a "weekly-report" skill connected (a weekly report: 3 blocks — done, problems, plans; business tone; a table with numbers). Write Claude a short request for the report — without restating all the rules, they're already in the skill.

steps.claudeReply:
Using the **weekly-report** skill.

Here's the report for the week in our format:

**Done:** closed 4 sprint tasks, updated the documentation.

**Problems:** delayed response from the contractor — risk of a 2-day slip in the timeline.

**Plans:** launch a test of the new form, meeting with the contractor on Wednesday.

| Metric | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Tasks in progress | 9 | 5 |

Notice: you wrote one line, and the structure, tone, and table came from the skill.
steps.failHint: A short request is enough, for example: "Put together the report for this week." The format rules already live in the skill — no need to repeat them.

tasks.successMessage: That's the whole point of skills: a short request — a full result following your rules. All the "how to do it" lives in SKILL.md, and Claude loaded it on its own.
